agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service serving predictions at P99 < 100ms during checkout. The design separates the hot prediction path (a lean inference service with pre-loaded models) from the cold training path (weekly batch retraining pipeline). Model versioning with instant rollback is achieved via a model registry with blue/green deployment. Given a 4-person team, the architecture favors managed services and proven patterns over custom infrastructure.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Inference Service","responsibility":"Serves real-time delivery time predictions. Loads the active model into memory at startup, accepts feature vectors over gRPC/HTTP, returns predictions. Stateless and horizontally scalable.","boundaries":"Owns only prediction logic and feature transformation at serving time. Does NOT train models, store raw data, or manage model lifecycle."},{"name":"Feature Store","responsibility":"Precomputes and serves features for both training and inference. Splits into an offline store (batch features for training) and an online store (low-latency key-value lookups for serving). Ensures training-serving feature parity.","boundaries":"Owns feature definitions and materialization. Does not own raw event ingestion or model logic."},{"name":"Training Pipeline","responsibility":"Weekly batch job that pulls labeled delivery data and features from the offline store, trains a new model candidate, evaluates it against validation metrics, and registers it in the Model Registry.","boundaries":"Runs offline. Never touches production traffic. Produces model artifacts only."},{"name":"Model Registry","responsibility":"Versioned storage of trained model artifacts with metadata (metrics, training data snapshot ID, timestamp). Supports tagging versions as 'active', 'candidate', or 'rollback'. Source of truth for which model version is live.","boundaries":"Storage and metadata only. Does not serve predictions or trigger deployments autonomously."},{"name":"Model Deployer","responsibility":"Orchestrates promotion of a candidate model to active. Runs shadow scoring or canary validation, then signals the Inference Service to load the new model. Handles rollback by re-pointing to the previous active version.","boundaries":"Deployment orchestration only. Does not train or evaluate models beyond go/no-go gate checks."},{"name":"Data Ingestion Pipeline","responsibility":"Captures completed delivery events (actual delivery times) from the logistics platform and writes them to the training data store. Handles deduplication, schema validation, and late-arriving data.","boundaries":"Owns raw data capture and cleaning. Does not compute features or train models."},{"name":"Monitoring & Alerting","responsibility":"Tracks prediction latency (P50/P95/P99), model accuracy drift (predicted vs actual delivery time MAE), feature drift, and throughput. Triggers alerts and can initiate automatic rollback if accuracy degrades beyond threshold.","boundaries":"Observability only. May trigger rollback via Model Deployer API but does not directly modify serving infrastructure."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Checkout Service (external)","to":"Inference Service","contract":"POST /predict {weight, distance, zone, time_of_day, weather, courier_load} → {estimated_minutes, confidence_interval, model_version}","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime-critical"},{"from":"Inference Service","to":"Feature Store (online)","contract":"GET features by key (zone, courier_id) → precomputed feature vector. Sub-5ms latency requirement.","coupling_type":"synchronous, runtime-critical"},{"from":"Inference Service","to":"Model Registry","contract":"On startup/signal: pull model artifact by tag 'active'. No runtime calls per-request.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, deployment-time"},{"from":"Training Pipeline","to":"Feature Store (offline)","contract":"Read training dataset with precomputed features as Parquet/batch query.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, batch"},{"from":"Training Pipeline","to":"Model Registry","contract":"PUT model artifact + metrics + metadata → new version tagged 'candidate'.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, batch"},{"from":"Model Deployer","to":"Model Registry","contract":"Read candidate version, promote tag to 'active', demote previous 'active' to 'rollback'.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, operational"},{"from":"Model Deployer","to":"Inference Service","contract":"POST /admin/reload-model → triggers hot-reload of active model from registry. Rolling restart if needed.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, operational"},{"from":"Data Ingestion Pipeline","to":"Feature Store (offline)","contract":"Write completed delivery events → materialized into training features on schedule.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, batch"},{"from":"Monitoring & Alerting","to":"Model Deployer","contract":"POST /rollback {reason} → triggers revert to previous model version.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, event-driven"}],"data_flow":"1. Completed deliveries flow through Data Ingestion → raw data store. 2. Feature Store materializes offline features nightly. 3. Weekly, Training Pipeline reads offline features + labels, trains model, registers candidate in Model Registry. 4. Model Deployer validates candidate (shadow scoring against recent actuals), promotes to active. 5. Inference Service hot-reloads new model. 6. At checkout time: Checkout Service → Inference Service. Inference Service enriches request with online features from Feature Store, runs inference, returns prediction in <100ms. 7. Monitoring compares predictions to actual delivery times (when available) and tracks drift.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Model Registry with Blue/Green Versioning","rationale":"Enables instant rollback by maintaining at least 2 model versions (active + previous). Rollback is a metadata tag flip, not a redeployment. Critical business requirement."},{"pattern":"Feature Store (Online/Offline split)","rationale":"Eliminates training-serving skew, the #1 cause of silent ML degradation. Online store serves precomputed features at low latency; offline store provides consistent features for training."},{"pattern":"Hot Model Reload","rationale":"Inference service loads model into memory and reloads on signal without restart. Avoids downtime during weekly model updates. Model is small enough (gradient boosted trees or small neural net) to fit in memory."},{"pattern":"Shadow Scoring / Canary Validation","rationale":"Before promoting a new model, run it against recent actual delivery data to compare MAE with the current active model. Prevents deploying a degraded model. Lightweight alternative to full A/B testing for a 4-person team."},{"pattern":"Strangler Pattern for ML Model Evolution","rationale":"Start with a simpler model (e.g., LightGBM) that easily meets latency requirements. Architecture allows swapping the model type without changing the serving contract, enabling future evolution to neural nets if needed."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Prediction Serving","classification":"core","aggregates":["PredictionRequest","PredictionResponse","ActiveModel"]},{"name":"Model Lifecycle","classification":"core","aggregates":["ModelVersion","TrainingRun","DeploymentEvent","RollbackEvent"]},{"name":"Feature Engineering","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["FeatureDefinition","MaterializationJob","OnlineFeatureSet"]},{"name":"Data Collection","classification":"generic","aggregates":["DeliveryEvent","DataQualityCheck"]},{"name":"Observability","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["PredictionLog","AccuracyMetric","DriftAlert"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Model Lifecycle","downstream":"Prediction Serving","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Model Lifecycle publishes model artifacts; Prediction Serving consumes them via the registry contract. Serving team can request model format/size constraints."},{"upstream":"Feature Engineering","downstream":"Prediction Serving","relationship":"Conformist. Inference service conforms to feature schema defined by Feature Engineering. Feature changes require coordinated updates."},{"upstream":"Data Collection","downstream":"Feature Engineering","relationship":"Open Host Service. Data Collection exposes raw delivery events via a stable schema. Feature Engineering transforms as needed."},{"upstream":"Observability","downstream":"Model Lifecycle","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Observability detects drift and can trigger rollback in Model Lifecycle."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Use a gradient-boosted tree model (LightGBM/XGBoost) as the initial model type","gains":"Sub-10ms inference latency (well within 100ms P99 budget). Small model size. Simple to train, debug, and explain. Feature importance is transparent for business stakeholders.","sacrifices":"May underperform deep learning on complex feature interactions. Less capacity to learn from raw unstructured inputs.","alternatives_considered":["Deep neural network — better accuracy ceiling but harder to meet latency, harder to debug, overkill for tabular features with 4-person team","Linear regression — too simple, won't capture zone/weather/load interactions well"]},{"decision":"Feature Store instead of real-time feature computation","gains":"Training-serving parity guaranteed. Online lookups are O(1) key-value reads. Clear ownership of feature logic.","sacrifices":"Operational overhead of maintaining a feature store. Features have staleness (materialization lag). Adds a dependency to the serving path.","alternatives_considered":["Compute features inline at request time — simpler initially but creates training-serving skew risk and adds latency","Pass all features from the caller — pushes complexity to checkout service, tight coupling"]},{"decision":"Weekly batch retraining rather than online/continuous learning","gains":"Simple, auditable, testable pipeline. Easy to validate before deployment. Matches 4-person team capacity.","sacrifices":"Model can be up to 7 days stale. Cannot adapt to sudden distribution shifts (e.g., flash storm) until next cycle.","alternatives_considered":["Daily retraining — more fresh but higher ops burden for marginal gain","Online learning — complex, hard to debug, risky for a small team"]},{"decision":"Shadow scoring validation instead of full A/B testing","gains":"No traffic splitting complexity. Can validate against historical actuals. Simple to implement and reason about.","sacrifices":"Doesn't capture real-world feedback loops (user behavior changes based on estimate). Less statistically rigorous.","alternatives_considered":["Full A/B test with traffic splitting — gold standard but requires traffic routing infrastructure and statistical expertise beyond 4-person team capacity","Manual evaluation — error-prone, doesn't scale"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Model algorithm","evolution_path":"Architecture is model-agnostic. Start with LightGBM, swap to neural net or ensemble later. The serving contract and feature interface remain stable."},{"area":"Feature set","evolution_path":"Feature Store allows adding new features (e.g., traffic data, holiday flags) without changing the serving layer. Add new feature definitions, materialize them, retrain."},{"area":"Retraining frequency","evolution_path":"Pipeline is parameterized by schedule. Move from weekly to daily to triggered-on-data-volume with a config change, no architectural rework."},{"area":"Serving infrastructure","evolution_path":"Inference Service is stateless. Can move from container-based deployment to serverless (with pre-warmed instances) or edge serving if latency requirements tighten."},{"area":"Multi-model serving","evolution_path":"Model Registry supports multiple active models. Can evolve to per-zone or per-region models by adding a routing layer in front of inference."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Prediction API contract","reason":"Checkout service integration point. Changing the request/response schema requires coordinated rollout with upstream consumers. Treat as a versioned API from day one."},{"area":"Feature Store online latency SLA","reason":"Feature Store lookups are on the critical path. Must remain sub-5ms. This constrains technology choices (Redis/DynamoDB, not Postgres) and feature complexity."},{"area":"Model artifact format","reason":"Inference Service and Training Pipeline must agree on serialization format. Standardize early (e.g., ONNX or native LightGBM binary) and version it."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Training-serving skew despite Feature Store","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement feature validation tests that compare feature distributions between training data and live serving logs. Run as part of the weekly training pipeline. Alert on statistical divergence."},{"risk":"P99 latency regression when model size grows","severity":"high","mitigation":"Enforce model size/complexity budget in the training pipeline. Gate deployment on latency benchmarks (run inference benchmark on candidate model before promotion). Set hard limit: model must serve P99 < 50ms to leave headroom."},{"risk":"Silent model degradation between retraining cycles","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Continuously compare predicted vs actual delivery times (once actuals are available, typically 30-120 min after prediction). Alert if MAE exceeds threshold. Auto-rollback if sustained."},{"risk":"Feature Store becomes single point of failure on serving path","severity":"high","mitigation":"Inference Service caches recent feature values locally with TTL. If Feature Store is unreachable, serve from cache with degraded freshness. Add circuit breaker. Feature Store itself should be multi-AZ Redis or equivalent."},{"risk":"Team bottleneck — 4 people across ML and platform","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Clear ownership split: ML engineers own Training Pipeline + Feature Engineering + Model evaluation. Platform engineers own Inference Service + Model Deployer + Feature Store infrastructure + Monitoring. Use managed services (e.g., AWS SageMaker Feature Store, MLflow for registry) to reduce custom infrastructure burden."},{"risk":"Rollback mechanism untested when needed","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Include rollback as a step in the weekly deployment runbook. Practice it monthly. Automate the full rollback path so it is a single command or API call."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Build the serving path first. Stand up Inference Service with a simple heuristic model (distance/zone lookup table) behind the prediction API contract. Integrate with checkout. This de-risks the latency requirement and API contract early. Phase 2 (Weeks 3-5): Build the training pipeline. Ingest historical delivery data, train first LightGBM model, register in Model Registry (MLflow). Deploy to Inference Service via hot-reload. Phase 3 (Weeks 5-7): Build Feature Store. Migrate from inline feature computation to online/offline store. Add shadow scoring validation. Phase 4 (Weeks 7-8): Monitoring, alerting, automated rollback. Harden for production.","files_to_create_or_modify":["inference-service/ — Python (FastAPI or gRPC) service. Single main.py with /predict endpoint, model_loader.py for hot-reload, feature_client.py for Feature Store integration.","training-pipeline/ — Python package. train.py (orchestrates training run), evaluate.py (validation metrics), register.py (pushes to MLflow).","feature-store/ — Feature definitions and materialization jobs. definitions/ for feature specs, materialize.py for batch computation, online_loader.py for populating online store.","model-registry/ — MLflow configuration or equivalent. mlflow_config.py, promotion scripts.","deploy/ — Kubernetes manifests or Terraform for infrastructure. inference-service deployment, feature-store (Redis), MLflow server.","monitoring/ — Prometheus metrics definitions, Grafana dashboards, alerting rules for latency and accuracy drift.","tests/ — Integration tests for prediction API latency benchmarks, feature parity tests, rollback smoke tests."],"patterns_to_follow":"Keep the inference service as thin as possible — it should do feature lookup, model.predict(), and return. No business logic. Use dependency injection for the model object so tests can swap in mock models. Feature definitions should be declared once and used for both offline materialization and online serving (single source of truth). Model artifacts must be immutable and versioned — never overwrite. Use structured logging with model_version in every prediction for debugging.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do not compute features differently in training vs serving — this is the most common ML system failure mode","Do not load the model from disk on every request — load once into memory, reload on signal","Do not skip the latency benchmark gate — a model that is accurate but slow will break checkout","Do not store model artifacts in git — use a proper artifact store (S3 + MLflow)","Do not build a custom feature store — use Redis with a thin abstraction layer or a managed service","Do not defer monitoring to 'later' — deploy prediction logging from day one so you have data to evaluate model quality","Do not over-engineer for this team size — 4 people cannot maintain a Kubeflow/Airflow/Feast/Seldon stack. Use simple cron + MLflow + FastAPI + Redis"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Serving architecture and latency design — well-understood problem with proven patterns","Model versioning and rollback mechanism — MLflow + blue/green is battle-tested","Training-serving split — clear separation is standard for ML systems","Team workload distribution — clean 2+2 split maps naturally to ML vs platform concerns"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact model algorithm performance — LightGBM is a strong default but actual accuracy depends on data quality and feature richness, which are unknown","Feature Store necessity at launch — might be premature for initial release; could start with inline features and add Feature Store in Phase 3 as recommended","Retraining frequency adequacy — weekly may be insufficient if delivery patterns shift rapidly (seasonal, weather events); need to validate with actual drift measurements"],"assumptions":["Checkout service can tolerate adding a synchronous dependency with 100ms budget","Historical delivery data with ground truth (actual delivery times) is available for initial training","Order volume is moderate (thousands to low tens-of-thousands per hour) — not millions per second","Weather and courier load data are available as real-time or near-real-time feeds","The logistics platform has existing infrastructure (Kubernetes or equivalent, object storage, basic monitoring)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service serving predictions at P99 < 100ms during checkout. The design separates the hot prediction path (a lean inference service with pre-loaded models) from the cold training path (weekly batch retraining pipeline). Model versioning with instant rollback is achieved via a model registry with blue/green deployment. Given a 4-person team, the architecture favors managed services and proven patterns over custom infras
